name: Boadicea Extended
description: Boadicea, also known as Boudicca, was the Queen of the Iceni tribe in Roman Britain. Her story is one of fierce resistance against Roman oppression. After her husband Prasutagus died, the Romans violated his will, seizing his lands and wealth. Boadicea was flogged and her daughters were raped, igniting a fire of rebellion that swept across southeastern Britain. Leading a coalition of Iceni, Trinovantes, and other tribes, she sacked Camulodunum (Colchester), Londinium (London), and Verulamium (St Albans), inflicting heavy casualties on the Roman forces. Her rebellion posed a serious threat to Roman control, nearly driving them out of Britain. Though ultimately defeated in battle, possibly near Mancetter, her courage and defiance made her a symbol of British resistance against foreign rule. Her legacy continues to inspire, representing female leadership, national pride, and the struggle for freedom. Boadicea's image has been romanticized and used to represent British national identity throughout history, particularly during the Victorian era. Her story is a testament to the power of resistance and the enduring spirit of the British people. She is remembered as a warrior queen who stood against the might of the Roman Empire, a figure of immense historical and cultural significance.
subtitle: Warrior Queen who defied Rome
achievements: Led a major revolt against Roman rule
Sacked and burned three Roman cities
Inflicted heavy casualties on Roman forces
Became a symbol of British resistance

keyMyths.title: The Widow's Revenge
keyMyths.description: After the death of her husband, the Romans seized Boadicea's lands and brutally mistreated her and her daughters, sparking her desire for revenge. This act of aggression fueled the Iceni rebellion against Roman rule.

keyMyths.title: The Sacking of Cities
keyMyths.description: Boadicea's forces successfully attacked and burned three major Roman cities: Camulodunum, Londinium, and Verulamium. These attacks demonstrated the strength of the rebellion and caused significant damage to Roman infrastructure and morale.

keyMyths.title: The Final Battle
keyMyths.description: Boadicea's rebellion was eventually crushed in a decisive battle against the Roman army led by Gaius Suetonius Paulinus. The exact location of the battle is unknown, but the Roman victory marked the end of the uprising.

sources.author: Tacitus
sources.description: A primary source providing a detailed account of Boadicea's rebellion from a Roman perspective.
sources.text: A primary source providing a detailed account of Boadicea's rebellion from a Roman perspective.

sources.author: Cassius Dio
sources.description: Another Roman historian who wrote about Boadicea's revolt, offering further insights into the events.

sources.description: A modern work exploring the role of women in Celtic society, including Boadicea.
